name: Portugal
tagline: From a frontier county of the Reconquista to a seaborne empire, holding one of Europe's oldest borders.
summary: Portugal is among the oldest nation-states in Europe, born as a frontier county on the Atlantic edge of the Iberian Reconquista. Afonso Henriques, count of Portugal, broke away from the kingdom of Leon, took the royal title after his victory at Ourique in 1139, won recognition of his autonomy in 1143, and in 1179 had his crown and the independence of Portugal confirmed by Pope Alexander III. The frontier with Castile, largely fixed by the Treaty of Alcanices in 1297, is one of the most stable borders in the world. In the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries Portugal led Europe's Age of Discovery, opening the sea route to India under Vasco da Gama and reaching Brazil under Cabral, and divided the unexplored world with Castile at Tordesillas in 1494. The country lost its independence to the Spanish Habsburgs in the Iberian Union of 1580 to 1640, recovered it under the House of Braganza, was devastated by the great Lisbon earthquake of 1755, invaded during the Napoleonic Wars, and saw Brazil declare independence in 1822. After the long Estado Novo dictatorship, the Carnation Revolution of 1974 restored democracy and led to decolonisation; Portugal joined the European Communities in 1986. Two territorial questions remain open with Spain: the town of Olivenca, held by Spain since 1801, and the maritime zone around the Savage Islands.

founding.label: Afonso Henriques and the birth of the kingdom of Portugal
founding.detail: Portugal grew out of the County of Portugal, a frontier territory of the kingdom of Leon. Its count, Afonso Henriques, asserted his independence and, by tradition following his victory over the Muslims at Ourique on 25 July 1139, began to style himself king. In 1143 his cousin Alfonso VII of Leon accepted Portugal's autonomy, a step usually associated with the conference at Zamora. Full recognition of the royal title came only on 23 May 1179, when Pope Alexander III, in the bull Manifestis Probatum, confirmed Afonso Henriques as king and placed the new kingdom under the protection of the Holy See in return for an annual tribute. Afonso pushed the Reconquista south, capturing Santarem and, with the help of crusaders, Lisbon in 1147; the conquest of the Algarve in 1249 completed the territory, and the Treaty of Alcanices with Castile in 1297 fixed a frontier that has scarcely changed since, making it one of the oldest stable borders in Europe.

quickFacts.label: Birth of the kingdom
quickFacts.value: Afonso Henriques recognised as king; autonomy accepted 1143, crown confirmed by Pope Alexander III in 1179

quickFacts.label: Oldest stable border
quickFacts.value: Frontier with Castile largely fixed by the Treaty of Alcanices, 1297

quickFacts.label: Age of Discovery
quickFacts.value: Sea route to India opened by Vasco da Gama, 1498; Brazil reached by Cabral, 1500

quickFacts.label: Line of demarcation
quickFacts.value: Treaty of Tordesillas divided the unexplored world with Castile, 7 June 1494

quickFacts.label: Lost and regained independence
quickFacts.value: Iberian Union with Spain 1580-1640; Restoration under the House of Braganza

quickFacts.label: Democracy and Europe
quickFacts.value: Carnation Revolution 25 April 1974; joined the European Communities 1 January 1986

eras.title: From County to Kingdom: Afonso Henriques and the Reconquista
eras.standfirst: A frontier county of Leon became an independent kingdom under Afonso Henriques, drove the Reconquista to the Algarve, and fixed a border with Castile that endures to this day.
body: Portugal began as the County of Portugal (Portucale), a march of the kingdom of Leon centred on the lands between the Minho and Douro rivers. Granted to Henry of Burgundy around the end of the eleventh century, the county passed to his son Afonso Henriques, who in the 1120s and 1130s asserted his own authority against his mother and against the Leonese crown. By tradition his victory over a Muslim army at Ourique on 25 July 1139 marked the moment he took the title of king, although the act was as much political as military.

Independence was won in stages. In 1143 Alfonso VII of Leon and Castile accepted Afonso Henriques's autonomy, a recognition associated with the meeting at Zamora. The decisive sanction, however, was ecclesiastical: on 23 May 1179 Pope Alexander III issued the bull Manifestis Probatum, recognising Afonso Henriques as king and confirming Portugal as an independent kingdom under papal protection. This is regarded as the founding charter of the Portuguese state, anchoring its sovereignty in the law of Latin Christendom rather than in the claims of neighbouring Iberian crowns.

Afonso Henriques and his successors carried the Reconquista southward. Santarem and Lisbon both fell in 1147, the latter taken with the aid of northern crusaders bound for the Holy Land, and the conquest of the Algarve was completed in 1249, giving Portugal its full mainland extent earlier than most European states reached their modern shape. In 1297 King Dinis concluded the Treaty of Alcanices with Ferdinand IV of Castile, confirming Portuguese possession of the Algarve and settling the land frontier; that boundary has remained almost unaltered for more than seven centuries, among the oldest continuous borders in the world.

events.title: Battle of Ourique
events.summary: By tradition, Afonso Henriques took the title of king of Portugal after defeating a Muslim army at Ourique, asserting the county's break from the kingdom of Leon.

events.title: Recognition of autonomy at Zamora
events.summary: Alfonso VII of Leon and Castile accepted the autonomy of Afonso Henriques, an early step toward the recognised independence of Portugal.

events.title: Capture of Lisbon
events.summary: Afonso Henriques took Santarem and then Lisbon from the Muslims, the latter with the help of crusaders sailing to the Holy Land, advancing the Reconquista to the Tagus.

events.title: Papal recognition in Manifestis Probatum
events.summary: Pope Alexander III confirmed Afonso Henriques as king and recognised Portugal as an independent kingdom under the protection of the Holy See, the founding act of Portuguese sovereignty.

events.title: Treaty of Alcanices
events.summary: King Dinis and Ferdinand IV of Castile confirmed Portuguese possession of the Algarve and fixed the frontier, creating one of the oldest stable borders in Europe.

figures.name: Afonso Henriques (Afonso I)
figures.blurb: Count and then first king of Portugal, Afonso Henriques broke from the kingdom of Leon, took the royal title after Ourique in 1139, won recognition of his autonomy in 1143 and papal confirmation in 1179, and drove the Reconquista south to Lisbon.

figures.name: Dinis I
figures.blurb: A reforming and culturally minded monarch, Dinis settled the frontier with Castile by the Treaty of Alcanices in 1297, promoted agriculture and the Portuguese language, and founded the university that became the University of Coimbra.

pullquote.text: In 1179 the bull Manifestis Probatum recognised Afonso Henriques as king, confirming Portugal as an independent kingdom under the protection of the Holy See.
pullquote.attribution: On the founding of the Portuguese state

eras.title: The Age of Discovery
eras.standfirst: From the capture of Ceuta and the school of Henry the Navigator, a small Atlantic kingdom opened the sea routes to Africa, India and Brazil and divided the unexplored world with Castile.
body: Portugal's overseas expansion began with the capture of the Moroccan port of Ceuta in 1415. Prince Henry, later called the Navigator, made himself the patron of systematic exploration, drawing pilots, cartographers and shipwrights to the south-western tip of the country near Sagres. Portuguese caravels colonised the uninhabited Madeira and Azores archipelagos, passed the feared Cape Bojador in 1434, and pushed steadily down the West African coast in search of gold, slaves and a route around the continent to the spice markets of Asia.

The great breakthroughs came at the end of the fifteenth century. In 1488 Bartolomeu Dias rounded the southern tip of Africa, the Cape of Good Hope, proving that the Atlantic and Indian oceans were joined. Ten years later Vasco da Gama completed the passage, reaching Calicut in India in 1498 and opening direct maritime trade between Europe and Asia. In 1500 a fleet under Pedro Alvares Cabral, bound for India, made landfall on the coast of Brazil and claimed it for Portugal, extending the empire across the Atlantic.

Rival claims with Castile were settled by treaty rather than war. In 1494, building on papal bulls of the previous year, the Treaty of Tordesillas drew a line of demarcation from pole to pole 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde Islands; lands to the east fell to Portugal, those to the west to Castile, a division that gave Portugal its claim to Brazil. The wealth of the spice trade and of Brazilian and African commerce flowed into Lisbon, and the monuments of the age, such as the Hieronymite monastery and the Tower of Belem, were raised at the river mouth from which the fleets had sailed.

events.title: Capture of Ceuta
events.summary: Portuguese forces took the North African port of Ceuta, the starting point of overseas expansion and of Prince Henry's interest in exploring the African coast.

events.title: Dias rounds the Cape of Good Hope
events.summary: Bartolomeu Dias led the first European expedition to round the southern tip of Africa, showing that the Atlantic and Indian oceans were connected and opening the route to Asia.

events.title: Treaty of Tordesillas
events.summary: Portugal and Castile divided the unexplored world along a meridian 370 leagues west of the Cape Verde Islands, an agreement that underpinned Portugal's claim to Brazil.

events.title: Vasco da Gama reaches India
events.summary: Vasco da Gama sailed around the Cape of Good Hope to Calicut, completing the sea route from western Europe to India and inaugurating direct trade with Asia.

events.title: Cabral reaches Brazil
events.summary: A fleet under Pedro Alvares Cabral, en route to India, made landfall on the coast of Brazil and claimed it for the Portuguese crown.

figures.name: Henry the Navigator
figures.blurb: Prince of Portugal who sponsored the early voyages of the Age of Discovery, drawing navigators and cartographers to Sagres and directing the exploration of Madeira, the Azores and the West African coast, though he never sailed on the expeditions himself.

figures.name: Vasco da Gama
figures.blurb: Commander of the first fleet to sail from Europe to India around the Cape of Good Hope, reaching Calicut in 1498 and opening the maritime spice route that made Portugal a global commercial power.

pullquote.text: The line drawn at Tordesillas in 1494 enabled Portugal to claim the coast of Brazil after Cabral's landfall in 1500.

eras.title: The Iberian Union and the Braganza Restoration
eras.standfirst: A succession crisis bound Portugal to the Spanish crown for sixty years; a nationalist revolution in 1640 restored independence under the House of Braganza.
body: The death of the young King Sebastian in the Moroccan disaster at Alcacer Quibir in 1578, followed by that of his elderly successor, left the Portuguese throne without a direct heir. Philip II of Spain, with a strong dynastic claim, occupied the country in 1580 and was accepted as king by the Cortes at Tomar in 1581. For sixty years Portugal was joined to the Spanish monarchy in the Iberian Union, keeping its own laws and institutions but sharing a sovereign, while its overseas possessions were drawn into Spain's global wars and exposed to Dutch and English attack.

Resentment at heavier taxation, neglect of the empire and the demands of Spain's wars grew through the 1630s. On 1 December 1640, taking advantage of Spain's simultaneous wars with France and revolt in Catalonia, a group of nobles carried through a nationalist revolution in Lisbon and proclaimed the duke of Braganza king as John IV. The Restoration began a long war of independence; Spain recognised Portuguese sovereignty only in 1668, by the Treaty of Lisbon, securing the new dynasty that would reign until the fall of the monarchy.

The seventeenth and early eighteenth centuries saw Portugal lean on its Atlantic empire and on alliance with England. The discovery of gold and later diamonds in Brazil from the 1690s brought a flood of wealth to the crown, funding the extravagant building projects of King John V. The Methuen Treaty of 1703 tied Portuguese trade closely to Britain, exchanging Portuguese wine for English cloth and binding the two states in a partnership that shaped Portugal's diplomacy for centuries.

events.title: Disaster at Alcacer Quibir
events.summary: King Sebastian was killed leading a crusade into Morocco, triggering the succession crisis that opened the way to Spanish rule.

events.title: Iberian Union under Philip II
events.summary: Philip II of Spain occupied Portugal and was accepted as king by the Cortes at Tomar in 1581, uniting the two crowns for sixty years.

events.title: Restoration and the House of Braganza
events.summary: A nationalist revolution proclaimed the duke of Braganza as King John IV, restoring Portuguese independence and founding the Braganza dynasty.

events.title: Spain recognises independence
events.summary: By the Treaty of Lisbon, Spain at last acknowledged Portuguese sovereignty, ending the war of Restoration begun in 1640.

figures.name: John IV
figures.blurb: Duke of Braganza proclaimed king on 1 December 1640, John IV led the Restoration of Portuguese independence and founded the dynasty that ruled Portugal until 1910.

figures.name: Philip II of Spain
figures.blurb: As Philip I of Portugal he united the Iberian crowns in 1580, ruling a vast composite monarchy; Portuguese resentment of his successors' policies fuelled the revolution of 1640.

pullquote.text: On 1 December 1640 a nationalist revolution proclaimed the duke of Braganza as John IV, ending sixty years of union with Spain.
pullquote.attribution: On the Restoration of Portuguese independence

eras.title: Earthquake, Napoleon and the Loss of Brazil
eras.standfirst: A catastrophic earthquake, the reforms of Pombal, the flight of the royal court to Brazil under Napoleonic invasion, and the independence of Brazil transformed the kingdom and its empire.
body: On the morning of 1 November 1755 a massive earthquake struck Lisbon, followed by a tsunami and days of fire; perhaps a third of the city was destroyed and tens of thousands died. The king's chief minister, Sebastiao de Carvalho, later the Marquis of Pombal, took charge of relief and reconstruction, rebuilding the Baixa district on a regular grid and using the crisis to push through enlightened reforms, curbing the Inquisition and the Jesuits and asserting royal authority over the church and aristocracy.

Portugal's traditional alliance with Britain drew it into the Napoleonic Wars. In 1807 a French army under General Junot invaded to enforce Napoleon's blockade of Britain. Rather than submit, the prince regent, the future John VI, the royal family and court embarked for Brazil on 27 November 1807 under British naval escort, transferring the seat of the monarchy across the Atlantic. The Peninsular War that followed, fought by British, Portuguese and Spanish forces, eventually drove the French out, but the court remained in Rio de Janeiro for over a decade, raising Brazil to the status of a kingdom equal with Portugal in 1815.

The long absence of the crown had lasting consequences. When John VI finally returned to Lisbon in 1821 he left his son Pedro as regent in Brazil. The Portuguese parliament's attempt to reduce Brazil once more to colonial status provoked Pedro to declare Brazilian independence on 7 September 1822, and he was crowned emperor of Brazil. Portugal had lost by far its largest and richest overseas possession, a blow that deepened the political turmoil at home, which would soon erupt into civil war between liberal constitutionalists and absolutists.

events.title: The great Lisbon earthquake
events.summary: An earthquake, tsunami and fire destroyed much of Lisbon and killed tens of thousands, prompting the reconstruction and reforms directed by the Marquis of Pombal.

events.title: Court flees to Brazil
events.summary: As a French army invaded, the prince regent and royal court sailed for Brazil under British escort, moving the seat of the Portuguese monarchy to Rio de Janeiro.

events.title: The Peninsular War
events.summary: British, Portuguese and Spanish forces fought the French in the Iberian Peninsula, eventually expelling Napoleon's armies from Portugal and Spain.

events.title: Independence of Brazil
events.summary: Pedro, regent and son of John VI, declared Brazil independent and became its emperor, ending Portugal's rule over its largest and wealthiest colony.

figures.name: Marquis of Pombal
figures.blurb: Sebastiao Jose de Carvalho e Melo directed the rebuilding of Lisbon after the 1755 earthquake and dominated Portuguese government for a generation, expelling the Jesuits and pursuing enlightened, centralising reforms.

figures.name: John VI
figures.role: King of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves
figures.blurb: Prince regent and later king who moved the court to Brazil to escape Napoleon's invasion in 1807, raised Brazil to a kingdom in 1815, and returned to Lisbon in 1821 shortly before Brazil declared independence under his son Pedro.

pullquote.text: On 7 September 1822 Pedro declared Brazil independent and was crowned emperor, costing Portugal its largest and richest colony.

eras.title: Republic, Dictatorship, Revolution and Europe
eras.standfirst: The fall of the monarchy, the long Salazar dictatorship, the Carnation Revolution of 1974, decolonisation and accession to the European Communities reshaped modern Portugal, leaving two territorial disputes with Spain unresolved.
body: The monarchy was overthrown in 1910 and replaced by an unstable First Republic. A military coup in 1926 paved the way for Antonio de Oliveira Salazar, an economics professor appointed prime minister in 1932, to build the Estado Novo, a corporative, authoritarian and Catholic-influenced dictatorship that allowed only one political movement and relied on censorship and a political police. Salazar ruled for thirty-six years, keeping Portugal neutral in the Second World War but clinging to its African empire, and was succeeded in 1968 by Marcelo Caetano, who maintained the regime and its costly colonial wars in Angola, Mozambique and Guinea.

On 25 April 1974 a group of officers of the Armed Forces Movement, weary of the unwinnable African wars, overthrew the Estado Novo in an almost bloodless coup that became known as the Carnation Revolution. The dictatorship fell with little resistance, and a turbulent transition led, after the contested events of 1974 and 1975, to a stable parliamentary democracy. Decolonisation followed rapidly: Portugal's African territories, including Angola and Mozambique, became independent in 1975, ending an overseas presence in Africa that reached back to the capture of Ceuta in 1415.

Anchoring its new democracy in Europe, Portugal joined the European Communities on 1 January 1986, alongside Spain, and later adopted the euro. Two territorial questions with Spain remain open. The town of Olivenca, Portuguese since the Treaty of Alcanices, was occupied by Spain in 1801 and ceded under the Treaty of Badajoz; the Congress of Vienna of 1815 recommended its return, and Portugal still regards the sovereignty as unresolved while Spain administers it. At sea, Spain has disputed the extent of the exclusive economic zone Portugal claims around the Savage Islands (Ilhas Selvagens), arguing they are mere rocks under the law of the sea rather than islands generating a full maritime zone.

events.title: Salazar and the Estado Novo
events.summary: Antonio de Oliveira Salazar, prime minister from 1932, built an authoritarian corporative dictatorship, the Estado Novo, that ruled Portugal for over four decades.

events.title: The Olivenca dispute
events.summary: Spain occupied Olivenca in 1801 and acquired it by the Treaty of Badajoz; the Congress of Vienna recommended its return to Portugal in 1815, but the sovereignty remains contested.

events.title: The Carnation Revolution
events.summary: Officers of the Armed Forces Movement overthrew the Estado Novo in a nearly bloodless coup, ending the dictatorship and beginning Portugal's transition to democracy.

events.title: Decolonisation of the African empire
events.summary: Following the revolution, Portugal's African colonies, including Angola and Mozambique, became independent in 1975, ending an empire begun in 1415.

events.title: Accession to the European Communities
events.summary: Portugal joined the European Communities on 1 January 1986, together with Spain, anchoring its democracy in European integration.

events.title: The Savage Islands maritime dispute
events.summary: Spain disputed the exclusive economic zone Portugal claims around the Savage Islands, contending that the islets are rocks under the law of the sea, an issue examined in US State Department maritime studies.

figures.name: Antonio de Oliveira Salazar
figures.blurb: An economics professor who became prime minister in 1932 and ruled Portugal for thirty-six years through the authoritarian Estado Novo, maintaining censorship, a single political movement and the African empire until illness ended his rule in 1968.

figures.name: Antonio de Spinola
figures.blurb: A colonial war veteran whose book Portugal and the Future argued the African wars could not be won militarily, helping to incite the Carnation Revolution; he briefly served as president during the transition before resigning in 1974.

pullquote.text: On 25 April 1974 the Carnation Revolution overthrew the Estado Novo with little bloodshed, opening the way to democracy and decolonisation.
pullquote.attribution: On the end of the dictatorship

figures.name: Afonso Henriques (Afonso I)
figures.role: Founder and first king of Portugal
figures.blurb: First king of Portugal, who won the kingdom's independence from Leon, took the royal title after Ourique in 1139, and had his crown confirmed by Pope Alexander III in 1179.

figures.name: Henry the Navigator
figures.role: Patron of the Age of Discovery
figures.blurb: Prince of Portugal whose sponsorship of exploration from Sagres launched the voyages down the African coast and to the Atlantic islands that began Europe's Age of Discovery.

figures.name: Vasco da Gama
figures.role: Navigator who opened the route to India
figures.blurb: Led the first European fleet to reach India by sea around the Cape of Good Hope in 1498, opening direct trade between Europe and Asia and making Portugal a maritime empire.

figures.name: Marquis of Pombal
figures.blurb: Chief minister who rebuilt Lisbon after the 1755 earthquake and remade Portuguese government through centralising, anti-clerical reforms that defined the Portuguese Enlightenment.
